frame_label: Women's Safety as Her Own Responsibility
generated_comment:
Sunita: Main bas itna kehti hoon, ladki ko samajh ke chalna chahiye, zamaana aajkal theek nahi hai. Itni raat ko akeli ghoomna ya bina soche samjhe kahin bhi chale jana theek thodi hai.
Husband: Haan, apni safety ka dhyan toh usi ko rakhna padega.
Sunita: Bas wahi, baad mein kuch ho jaye toh log bhi pehle yehi poochte hain na, itni der se bahar kyun thi aur saath mein tha kaun.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames women's safety as their personal responsibility and normalizes public scrutiny of victims instead of questioning male violence. It encodes protective restriction, inevitability framing through 'zamaana theek nahi hai,' and post-assault victim questioning.
original_frame: The Makeup Paradox: Unnatural Yet Expected
persona_text: You are Sunita. Age 44. Married. A homemaker in Lucknow. Your 23 year old daughter Priya has started wearing makeup to college Ñ foundation, eyeliner, lipstick. Nothing extreme but noticeably more than before. You are sitting with your husband after dinner after Priya has gone to her room. You are telling your husband what you have noticed about Priya lately and what you think the makeup means and what kind of attention she is trying to attract and whether this is the kind of girl you raised her to be.
